metadata.career_relevance: Bounded type parameters are how professional generic APIs enforce contracts. `<T extends Comparable<T>>` means 'give me any type that can be sorted'. `<T extends Number>` means 'give me any numeric type'. `Collections.sort()`, `Collections.min()`, `TreeMap<K extends Comparable<K>>` — all use bounds. After this topic, you understand WHY these signatures look the way they do, and you can write the same kind of constrained generic APIs.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll use `extends` to bound type parameters (both on classes and methods), call methods from the bound interface/class on T, use `Comparable` and `Number` bounds for the most common use cases, combine multiple bounds with `&`, and understand why Java's utility methods have those complex-looking signatures.

visual_aid.description: Show bound syntax as a Venn diagram: all types (large circle), inside it 'Types extending Number' (medium circle containing Integer, Double, Long, Float, BigDecimal), inside that 'Types extending Comparable' (overlapping). The intersection shows what `<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>` accepts. Below: before/after comparison — without bound: T can be anything, calling compareTo() fails; with bound: T constrained, compareTo() allowed. Side: class hierarchy showing Integer → Number → Object, Integer → Comparable<Integer>. Multiple bounds rule: class first, then interfaces, separated by &.
visual_aid.alt_text: Bounded type parameter Venn diagram, before/after with/without bounds, and Number hierarchy

worked_example.scenario: Build a `Statistics<T>` class that computes summary statistics on any numeric type. Uses `<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>` to get both numeric operations (doubleValue) and comparison operations (compareTo) on the same T.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Create `Statistics<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>` with:
- Add data points with `add(T)`
- Compute: `sum()`, `average()`, `min()`, `max()`, `count()`
- Range: `range()` = max - min
- `isBetween(T value)` — is value within [min, max]?

Test with Integer data and Double data.
worked_example.expected_output:
Exam scores:
  Count: 6
  Average: 84.33333333333333
  Min: 68 | Max: 95
  Range: 27.0
  90 in range: true

Stock prices:
  Average: ₹2078.0
  Range: ₹3478.0
things_to_notice: The bound `<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>` grants BOTH sets of methods: Number's `doubleValue()` for arithmetic AND Comparable's `compareTo()` for ordering. Without both bounds, you'd only have one.
Inside the class, T behaves as if it IS a Number AND a Comparable<T>. The compiler knows which methods are available.
The same Statistics<T> class works for Integer, Double, Long, Float — any numeric type that also implements Comparable. One class handles them all, type-safely.
Statistics<String> would be a COMPILE ERROR — String extends Comparable<String> but NOT Number. The & ensures both bounds are satisfied.
range() uses both: `max().doubleValue()` (Number) minus `min().doubleValue()` (Number). Both max() and min() used compareTo() (Comparable) internally.

industry_examples.use_case: `Collections.sort(List<T>)` requires `<T extends Comparable<? super T>>`. `TreeMap<K, V>` requires K extends Comparable to keep keys sorted. These are the most-used bounded generics in Java — every time you use TreeMap or sort a List, bounds are enforcing that the elements are orderable.

Spring Data's `CrudRepository<T, ID extends Serializable>` — the primary key type ID must extend Serializable. `JpaRepository` has `<T, ID extends Comparable<ID>>` in some implementations. Bounds ensure primary keys satisfy the required contracts (serializable, comparable for range queries).

industry_examples.company_context: Financial calculation libraries (Zerodha, Razorpay, HDFC internal)
industry_examples.use_case: Internal financial libraries have `<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>` for portfolio analytics (Statistics<Double> for returns, Statistics<Long> for volumes, Statistics<Integer> for counts). One generic statistics class serves all numeric domains without code duplication.

applications.interview_relevance:
Bounded generics questions at senior level:

- **'What does `<T extends Comparable<T>>` mean?'** — T must implement Comparable<T>. The compiler allows calling compareTo() on T inside the method/class. Prevents using the method with non-comparable types.
- **'Why does TreeMap require its key type to extend Comparable?'** — TreeMap keeps keys sorted. To sort, it must be able to call compareTo(). The bound enforces this contract at compile time.
- **'What is the difference between `<T extends Number>` and `<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>`?'** — First: only Number methods available. Second: BOTH Number AND Comparable methods available, but T must satisfy both constraints.
- **'Can you write a generic min() method?'** → `static <T extends Comparable<T>> T min(List<T> list)`
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily bounded generics:
- Writing a utility that compares values: add `<T extends Comparable<T>>`
- Writing a numeric utility: add `<T extends Number>`
- Writing a value class that should support sorting: implement `Comparable<T>` on the class, then `SortedBox<T extends Comparable<T>>` holds it
- Reading a Stack Overflow answer that has `<T extends Comparable<? super T>>` — now you understand the base (extends Comparable), wildcards come in Topic 9.4

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using `implements` instead of `extends` in the type bound — `<T implements Comparable<T>>` doesn't compile.
common_gaps.remediation: In generics, ALWAYS use `extends` for bounds — even for interfaces. `<T extends Comparable<T>>`, `<T extends Iterable<T>>`, `<T extends Number>` — always `extends`, never `implements`.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compile error: 'implements' cannot be used as a bound

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Putting a class bound after an interface bound: `<T extends Comparable<T> & Number>` — compile error.
common_gaps.remediation: Class bounds must come FIRST: `<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>`. Then interfaces. At most one class, any number of interfaces, class first.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Compile error: 'The type Number is not an interface; it cannot be specified as a bounded parameter'

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Knowing the syntax but not connecting it to method availability inside the class/method.
common_gaps.remediation: The POINT of a bound is to use methods from it. `<T extends Comparable<T>>` → you can call `item.compareTo(other)` on any T inside. `<T extends Number>` → you can call `item.doubleValue()`. Write the bound, then USE the methods it unlocks.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Correct bound, but no use of the bound's methods inside the body

key_takeaways: Upper bound: `<T extends SomeType>` — T must extend/implement SomeType
In generics, `extends` means both extends (class) AND implements (interface)
The bound grants access to SomeType's methods inside the generic scope
Most common bound: `<T extends Comparable<T>>` — enables compareTo() for sorting
Number bound: `<T extends Number>` — enables doubleValue(), intValue() for arithmetic
Multiple bounds: `<T extends ClassBound & Interface1 & Interface2>` — class FIRST
Class bound: `class SortedBox<T extends Comparable<T>>` — ALL instances must use Comparable types
If T doesn't satisfy the bound at the call site → COMPILE ERROR (not runtime)
Collections.sort, Collections.min/max, TreeMap all use Comparable bounds
Statistics<T extends Number & Comparable<T>> — the archetypal dual-bound pattern

self_check_questions.question: What does `<T extends Comparable<T>>` allow you to do inside a method that plain `<T>` doesn't?
self_check_questions.answer: Call `item.compareTo(other)` on any T. Without the bound, T could be anything — the compiler doesn't know T has compareTo(). With the bound, the compiler guarantees T implements Comparable<T>, so compareTo() is always available. This unlocks: sorting, finding min/max, range checks, ordering any T.

self_check_questions.question: Why does `<T extends Comparable<T> & Number>` fail to compile, but `<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>` works?
self_check_questions.answer: Multiple bounds require the CLASS bound first, then INTERFACES. Comparable is an interface; Number is a class. In `<T extends Comparable<T> & Number>`, the class Number comes after an interface — illegal. In `<T extends Number & Comparable<T>>`, Number (class) is first, Comparable (interface) is second — correct.

self_check_questions.question: Write the signature for a generic method `clamp(value, min, max)` that works for any Comparable type.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **Wildcards** — `?`, `? extends T`, `? super T`. When you want to read a `List<Integer>` or `List<Double>` with one method, wildcards let you say 'a List of anything that extends Number': `List<? extends Number>`. The distinction between wildcards and bounded type parameters is subtle but important — wildcards are for USE SITES (parameters), bounds are for DEFINITION SITES (class/method declarations). After Topic 9.4, Java generic signatures that once looked cryptic — `Collection<? extends T>`, `Comparator<? super T>` — will be completely clear.
